pattern: Sorting by width ascending and breaking width ties by descending height collapses the two-dimensional nesting into a one-dimensional longest strictly increasing subsequence on the heights.
hints: Nesting needs both width and height to strictly increase, so first impose an order on width and then worry only about height.
If two envelopes share a width, neither can contain the other, so on equal widths sort heights in decreasing order to stop them being chosen together.
After that sort, the answer is the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence of the height sequence, computable in n log n with patience sorting.

edge_inputs.description: classic chain of three nesting envelopes
all identical, none can nest, answer is one
equal widths with increasing heights must not chain
equal heights with increasing widths must not chain
strictly increasing in both dimensions all nest
reverse order chain still found after sorting

stress.description: many random envelopes with frequent width ties to exercise the tie-breaking rule
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    env = [[rng.randint(1, 60), rng.randint(1, 200)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [env]
stress.note: narrow width range guarantees many ties so the descending-height rule is tested
